tests.title: Verbs of Motion
tests.description: Prefixes, aspect, and foot vs. vehicle — fill in the blank with the right motion verb form.

questions.fb: «Завтра» + one specific trip = perfective. По- signals the start of a single movement. You're going on foot, so it's the идти family → пойду.
«Каждый день» is your habit alarm — always imperfective. Bus = vehicle, so ездить (habitual, no prefix). Едет would mean she's on the bus right now, in this moment.
After «когда» in a future chain, Russian uses perfective — the return has to be DONE before the call happens. Sequence logic, not future tense.
«Долго» = ongoing duration in the past. That's imperfective. Taxi = vehicle → ехал (the ride was in progress, not a completed event).

tags: за- = stop by on the way
questions.fb: За- specifically means 'stop by while passing.' You're not just entering (в-) or exiting (вы-) — you're making a quick detour on a route. Classic за-.
«Каждое утро» = habit → imperfective. Вы- means going from inside to outside. Present tense habitual = выходит.
Train = vehicle. One specific trip tomorrow = perfective. По- + vehicle family = поедем. Compare: пойдём would mean you're walking to Moscow.
«Каждый час» = repeated event → imperfective. Про- means passing by or through something. On foot → проходят.

s: к нам в гости на прошлой неделе.
questions.fb: При- = arrival. Here's a subtle one: приходил (ipfv past) implies he came AND left — the whole visit is packaged as a round trip. Пришёл (pfv) would focus on the moment of arrival itself.
Right now, in this moment, in one direction = directed imperfective (едет). This is the 'camera is on it happening' form. Ездит would mean the car drives here regularly.
Two quick completed actions in sequence — he exited, then closed the door. Sequence of completed events = perfective. Вышел, then закрыл.

s: на велосипеде на работу каждый день.
questions.fb: «Раньше» + «каждый день» = habitual past → imperfective. Bicycle = vehicle → ездил. (Ехал would mean you were mid-ride, not describing a habit.)
Пере- = crossing or relocating. After «когда окончу» the chain of events continues with perfective. Moving to a new city by definition involves transport → переехать.
При- = arrival. One specific arrival, in ten minutes → perfective. Walking (already established in context) → foot family → приду.
До- means reaching a specific endpoint. «Увидел» after it means the reaching was a completed event that triggered something else — classic perfective chain. Foot → дошёл.
Relocating to a city = пере- prefix. Future, one event = perfective. Moving cities = transport → переехать. Don't use перейти — that's crossing on foot (crossing a street, crossing a border).
Boat = vehicle, so переехать. The crossing was a completed event (then it was done) → perfective. Note: перешли would mean crossing on foot.
С- (сходить) is a special prefix meaning 'make a quick round trip' — go there and come back. It's efficient and colloquial. Пойду just means 'I'll go' with no implication of return.
От- = moving away from a point (the stop). It was a completed departure at a specific moment (while you were running) → perfective. Bus = vehicle → отъехал.
«Всегда буду» = always will (habitual future) → imperfective. You're not promising one arrival — you're promising a pattern. Буду + imperfective infinitive = habitual future.
